YUAN-LQC-VAZ-PHX-020  ·  ro-yuan-020

Yuan Longquan Celadon Baluster Vase with Phoenix Handles

元代龙泉窑青瓷凤首耳瓶 — Yuándài Lóngquán Yáo Qīngcí Fèng Shǒu Ěr Píng

Longquan Celadon 龙泉青瓷 Yuan Dynasty · 1271–1368 CE Longquan Kilns, Zhejiang Province Baluster Vase · Phoenix Handles Reduction-Fired Stoneware Dronova GOM Method

Object Description

This baluster vase with phoenix-form handles represents a rare and technically accomplished example of Longquan celadon production during the Yuan dynasty. The vessel presents an elongated ovoid body rising from a low spreading foot to a short constricted neck and widely flared mouth rim — proportions that correspond closely to Yuan Longquan ceremonial vessels preserved in the Palace Museum, the Metropolitan Museum of Art, and the British Museum. The form is distinguished from contemporaneous Song Longquan production by its greater overall scale, more emphatic shoulder, and the integration of sculptural zoomorphic handles.

The paired phoenix handles are among the most iconographically significant features of this vessel. Each handle takes the form of a stylised phoenix (fènghuáng, 鳳凰) with an elongated curved neck, simplified but distinctive wing silhouette, and an S-curved body that bridges the shoulder to the upper neck. The birds are hand-modelled and individually finished, with subtle asymmetries between the two handles confirming manual rather than mould-pressed production. Phoenix-form handles are uncommon within the Longquan repertoire and are generally associated with higher-status ceremonial or ritual vessels of the Yuan period.

The glaze is a deep, rich olive-green celadon of the type characteristic of mature Yuan Longquan production — thicker and more saturated in colour than Song-period precedents, with a pronounced layered optical depth and darker pooling in the recessed areas around the handles and at the neck-shoulder junction. A natural irregular craquelure network covers the entire glazed surface, denser and with finer cells on the body, wider and more open near the neck, with iron-stained "blood crackle" visible around the mouth rim where the thinner glaze allows greater iron migration.

Phoenix iconography: During the Yuan dynasty, phoenix imagery expanded beyond its Song-period association with imperial consorts and appeared on selected Longquan ritual vessels for elite and Buddhist monastic patrons. The phoenix symbolises imperial virtue, cosmic harmony, renewal, and auspicious power — transforming this functional vessel into a charged symbolic object appropriate to ceremonial display and offering contexts.

Microscopic Analysis (GOM)

Геммологическая оптическая микроскопия проводилась по Методу Дроновой при увеличениях ×10, ×20, ×40 в режимах отражённого, косого и тёмнопольного освещения. Приоритетные зоны: поверхность глазури на тулове (кракле и микроструктура) и подножие (граница глазурь/черепок).

Zone A — Глазурь и кракле, ×40

GOM · ×40
×40

Микрогранулярная поверхность глазури с полукристаллическим видом. Газовые пузырьки мелкие, неравномерного размера — признак высокотемпературного восстановительного обжига. Стенки кракле с железным окрашиванием. Включения железа вблизи границы глазурь/черепок.

При ×40 глазурь лунцюань демонстрирует характерную микрогранулярную текстуру с полукристаллическим видом и мелкими газовыми пузырьками неравномерного размера — диагностический признак высокотемпературного восстановительного обжига. Стенки кракле окрашены оксидом железа — «кровавое кракле» (xuècái, 血彩), признак длительного естественного старения без искусственного введения красителя. Дискретные железистые включения вблизи границы глазурь/черепок обусловливают характерную оптическую глубину лунцюаньской селадоновой глазури.

Zone B — Подножие, граница глазурь/черепок

Макро · ×10
×10 Macro

Граница глазури: естественное скругление края и истончение у подножия. Красно-коричневый черепок с зольным осадком печи. Следы обрезки колесом. Признаков реглазирования нет.

Граница глазури у подножия демонстрирует характерное для лунцюаньского производства естественное скругление и истончение без признаков реглазирования. Красно-коричневый незаглазурованный черепок с зольным осадком печи и следами обрезки колесом соответствует телу высокообожжённого стоунвера печей Лунцюань провинции Чжэцзян.

Diagnostic Interpretation

Объект оценивался по девятикритериальной диагностической матрице Метода Дроновой для юаньских лунцюаньских селадонов. Все критерии удовлетворены с высоким уровнем достоверности.

Diagnostic Category Observed Assessment
MorphologyBaluster form, flared mouth, low spreading foot — canonical Yuan Longquan proportions✓ Strong Positive
BodyDense reddish-brown high-fired stoneware; compact fabric; no modern industrial porosity✓ Strong Positive
GlazeThick olive-green celadon; high transparency; strong optical depth; darker pooling in recesses✓ Strong Positive
CrackleIrregular geometry; variable cell size; iron-stained "blood crackle" at mouth; denser on body✓ Strong Positive
Phoenix handlesHand-modelled sculptural attachments; subtle asymmetry; S-curved body; individual finish✓ Rare Yuan type
FootUnglazed reddish-brown body; kiln ash residue; wheel trimming marks; natural vitrification✓ Strong Positive
Microscopy (GOM)Microgranular semi-crystalline glaze; fine bubbles; iron inclusions at glaze-body boundary✓ Positive
TechnologyReduction firing; thick iron glaze; traditional wheel throwing; hand-trimmed foot; kiln ash✓ Strong Positive
Comparative analysisClose correspondence with Palace Museum, Metropolitan Museum, British Museum examples✓ Strong Positive

Diagnostic Checklist

  • Deep olive-green Longquan celadon glaze with characteristic optical depth — primary Longquan identification criterion
  • Natural irregular craquelure with iron-stained "blood crackle" at mouth rim — centuries of authentic ageing, no artificial staining
  • Hand-modelled phoenix handles with individual asymmetry — confirms manual production, not mould-pressed reproduction
  • Dense reddish-brown stoneware body — diagnostic for Longquan kiln iron-bearing paste, Zhejiang Province
  • Unglazed foot with kiln ash residue and wheel trimming marks — authentic kiln firing evidence
  • GOM: microgranular semi-crystalline glaze with fine irregular bubbles — high-temperature reduction firing signature
  • Iron inclusions at glaze-body boundary contributing to optical depth — consistent with Longquan multi-application glaze technique
  • Natural glaze edge thinning at foot — single-firing signature, no evidence of reglaze or secondary layer
  • No evidence of modern restoration, reglaze, artificial ageing, or reproduction characteristics detected

Diagnostic Fingerprint

Authentication at a glance — полный диагностический портрет объекта.

Diagnostic MarkerAuthentic Yuan Longquan Example
BodyDense reddish-brown stoneware
GlazeThick olive-green celadon
CrackleNatural irregular, blood crackle
Phoenix handlesHand-modelled, S-curved
Diagnostic MarkerAuthentic Yuan Longquan Example
FootUnglazed, kiln ash, reddish body
GOM glazeMicrogranular, fine bubbles
Iron inclusionsAt glaze-body boundary
Museum comparandaPalace Museum, Met, British Museum
Сочетание ручного моделирования ручек-фениксов с индивидуальной асимметрией, железного «кровавого кракле» на устье и микрогранулярной полукристаллической глазури при ×40 составляет диагностическую триаду, специфичную для высокостатусных ритуальных сосудов лунцюаньской традиции юаньского периода. Все три критерия удовлетворяются в данном образце.

Cross-References

ro-song-011 — Qingbai Ewer with Carved Peonies

Celadon glaze comparison across Song-Yuan traditions: Song qingbai (transparent calcium-rich, low bubbles) vs Yuan Longquan celadon (thick olive-green, iron-rich, microgranular). Both represent the finest celadon traditions of their respective periods.

→ View ro-song-011

ro-yuan-017 — Yuan Black-Glazed Meiping with Dragon

Contrast in Yuan glaze aesthetics: deep iron-black reduction glaze (ro-yuan-017) vs deep olive-green celadon (ro-yuan-020). Both exemplify the mastery of iron-rich reduction-fired glazes in Yuan ceramic production.

→ View ro-yuan-017

ro-yuan-019 — Yuan Double-Gourd Ewer with Iron Splashes

Iron oxide in glaze comparison within the Yuan period: scattered iron splashes on pale ochre base (ro-yuan-019) vs homogeneous iron-rich olive celadon (ro-yuan-020). Two distinct approaches to iron-oxide glaze aesthetics in southern Yuan kilns.

→ View ro-yuan-019
